title: Acceptance and Constraints
desc: A practical look at how identifying your coordinates allows you to build solutions.
body:
One of the biggest lessons I learned growing up with Friedrich's ataxia is that acceptance is the foundation of strength.

When my coordination began changing, I spent time fighting the reality of it. Designing workarounds begins with seeing our coordinates clearly.

"Identifying constraints is the first step of construction. It is finding the clearest path on your coordinates."

In digital design, constraints are normal. We deal with code limits and screen boundaries. We design responsive layouts to fit the exact parameters available. When you treat physical or personal limitations as simple parameters, you focus your energy on what you can build today.

title: The Skydiving Tape Method
desc: How a roll of tape and a jump from 14,000 feet taught me the reality of daily adaptation.
body:
August 27, 2021. I was scheduled to jump out of a airplane. After monsoon rains delayed us three times, the skies cleared and we got the green light to fly.

Just before boarding, my tandem expert realized a problem: 'We need you to lift and hold your legs up for landing so they don't break. Since you can't control them, we have a problem.'

He stayed calm and focused on a workaround. He ran to the supply cabinet and returned with a roll of white duck-tape. He taped my legs together so I could lift them as a single unit using my upper body strength. It worked perfectly.

"When life gives you coordination blocks, find the nearest roll of duck-tape."

That jump taught me that adaptation is gritty, immediate, and practical. When you want to build a solution, you work with the available parameters to design a bridge.

title: How to Build a Voice Companion Avatar
desc: A step-by-step breakdown on how I trained ChelloAI on my personal guidelines and voice parameters to help write for me.
body:
Typing is a physical struggle. As coordination changes, pushing keys is exhausting. That's why I use AI models as my typing assistants.

ChelloAI was born out of a simple need: I needed a digital helper that could communicate my thoughts in my voice. To build it, I compiled a text corpus detailing my life timeline, my design experiences, and specific conversational parameters.

The rules I used to train the companion focus on keeping the writing authentic, avoiding marketing buzzwords, and framing challenges positively without seeking pity. By loading this custom data, I now have an assistant that speaks with my direct attitude, acting as a conversational partner.

title: The W.I.N. Reframe Matrix
desc: A practical reflection tool to map out physical, strategic, or budget constraints and decide what outcome to build next.
body:
Whether you are a business owner dealing with a tight budget or an individual dealing with a health coordinate, we all face boundaries. I created the W.I.N. framework to audit these limits and find direction.

The matrix is split into three phases:

  1. Warrior Story: Write down the limitations you are facing. Acknowledge them fully as parameters.
  2. Inspiring Impact: Identify the highest value outcome you can produce within these coordinates.
  3. Nurturing Outcomes: Break down the daily steps. Use AI tools to handle repetitive tasks and save your energy.

By moving through this matrix, you focus your energy on what you can create from where you are today.

title: Finding My Current: Consistency in a Shifting Summer
desc: Embracing our limitations as fixed design parameters allows us to build consistent momentum, even when the world shifts around us.
body:
June 26th. The summer heat is really settling in here in Florida. It’s the kind of day where the air feels thick, and you can almost see the humidity shimmering off the pavement. It reminds me of those subtle currents in the ocean – sometimes you barely notice them, but if you don’t pay attention, you can drift far from where you intended to be. Lately, I’ve been doing a lot of mid-year reflection, looking back at the ambitious plans I laid out for 2026.

How many of us started the year with a surge of energy, only to find ourselves now, halfway through, paddling against an unexpected tide or feeling the pull of a current that wants to take us off course? This time of year, with summer in full swing and the initial 'new year' motivation long past, is often when consistency becomes a real challenge. It's easy to lose momentum.

For me, living with Friedrich's ataxia, consistency isn't just a good habit; it's a non-negotiable requirement for daily life. My body demands a certain rhythm, a specific pacing. I can’t simply 'push through' days when my energy is low or my balance is off. There are parameters I must respect. This isn’t a weakness; it’s simply a fact. And crucially, it's a design parameter.

This shift in perspective has been monumental for me. Instead of fighting against what my body can or cannot do, I now approach it like an engineer. If I’m building a bridge, I don’t complain about the tensile strength of steel; I work within it. If I’m designing a circuit, I understand the voltage limits. My physical reality, with Friedrich's ataxia, provides a set of fixed parameters. The question then becomes: Given these parameters, how do I build something meaningful? How do I keep moving forward, consistently, with purpose?

This reframing turns what could be a source of frustration into a creative challenge. It means I often lean heavily on the incredible AI tools at my disposal. Voice-to-text software allows me to write even on days when my hands are less cooperative. AI assistants help me organize complex thoughts and manage my schedule to account for necessary rest periods, ensuring I don't overcommit. These aren't 'crutches' in a diminishing sense; they are integral parts of my personal design solution, allowing me to maintain my output and stay consistent, even when my physical energy fluctuates.

My faith also plays a crucial role here. Trusting that there's purpose and a path forward, even within these defined parameters, keeps me anchored. It allows me to accept the currents of life and still navigate them with hope.

So, here’s a challenge for you today: Where in your own life are you fighting against a 'fixed parameter' instead of designing around it? Maybe it’s a time constraint at work, a personal responsibility, or a perceived skill gap. Instead of seeing it as a blocker, what if you embraced it as a non-negotiable element of your project or your day? How would that change your strategy?

Try it. Pick one area. Reframe the limitation as a design parameter. Then, take one small, consistent action based on that new perspective. Momentum isn't always about speed. Often, it's about persistent, deliberate movement, adapting to the current, and trusting the journey. Keep building, keep moving. God bless.
